summary: Fowler responds to criticism that patterns books offer nothing new to experienced developers by arguing this misses the point entirely. Patterns are meant to capture existing field knowledge, not present original ideas. The real value of patterns books lies in their role as communication tools, helping experienced developers transfer knowledge to less experienced team members. Since few teams consist entirely of seasoned developers, patterns provide a shared vocabulary for mentorship and skill transfer.
key_points: Patterns deliberately capture existing knowledge rather than presenting original ideas
The criticism that patterns books teach nothing new to experts actually validates their purpose
Patterns serve as communication tools between experienced and less experienced developers
Knowledge transfer is one of the most important responsibilities of experienced team leaders
money_quotes: Not just is this true, but it's the whole point of patterns.
Patterns are there to capture knowledge from the field, not to present original ideas.
One of the most important things an experienced leader can do is pass on her skills.
Few teams consist of entirely of seasoned developers.
key_insight: Patterns exist not to teach experts new concepts, but to give them a shared vocabulary for transferring their hard-won knowledge to others.
